questions.q: What does 'exactly-once' semantics mean in a streaming context?
options: Each event is processed and contributes to state exactly once, even if the system fails and recovers
The streaming system can only process one event at a time
Events can only be read from a stream exactly one time before being deleted
Exactly one consumer can read from a Kafka topic at any moment
questions.explanation: Exactly-once semantics guarantee that despite system failures, restarts, or retries, each input event affects the result exactly once. This requires idempotent operations and either deduplication (recognizing duplicate delivery) or distributed transactions. Kafka and Spark Structured Streaming support exactly-once with proper configuration.

questions.q: How does Kafka maintain fault tolerance in a distributed streaming architecture?
options: By replicating partitions across brokers and maintaining an offset log that allows consumers to resume from any point
Kafka stores all data in memory and has no durability guarantees
By preventing any broker from failing through active health monitoring
Kafka requires manual intervention to recover from any broker failure
questions.explanation: Kafka replicates each partition to multiple brokers (default replication factor 3), tolerating broker failures. Consumer offsets are stored, allowing applications to pause and resume without losing position. When a consumer restarts, it reads its committed offset and continues processing.

questions.q: What is the difference between event time and processing time in stream processing?
options: Event time is when the event occurred at source; processing time is when the system processed it—they can differ due to latency and out-of-order events
They are the same thing described with different terminology
Event time is used only in batch processing; processing time only in streaming
Processing time is always before event time in a streaming system
questions.explanation: A user clicks at 3:00 PM (event time) but network delays mean the system processes it at 3:05 PM (processing time). Windowing should typically use event time for accurate analytics. Event time windows are called 'tumbling' windows while processing time windows are called 'wall-clock' windows.

questions.q: What problem does watermarking solve in streaming systems?
options: Tracking how far behind real-time the system is and deciding when to close windows despite late-arriving data
Preventing sensitive data from being stored in stream topics
Compressing data as it flows through the pipeline
Ensuring that stream partitions are evenly sized
questions.explanation: A watermark is a timestamp threshold that represents when the system believes it has seen all events up to that time. If the watermark reaches 3:05 PM, the system assumes no events from before 3:05 PM will arrive. This allows windows to close and emit results. The watermark lags by a heuristic duration to handle expected delays.

questions.q: In stream processing, what is backpressure and why does handling it matter?
options: When incoming data rate exceeds processing rate, and systems must buffer or slow input to prevent memory exhaustion and data loss
The pressure exerted by downstream systems on upstream producers
A technique for accelerating stream processing by discarding events
An indicator that the stream topic has too many partitions
questions.explanation: Backpressure occurs when events arrive faster than they can be processed. Without backpressure handling, buffers fill and memory runs out. Solutions include pause/resume signaling (Kafka), bounded queues that block producers, or scaling consumers. Ignoring backpressure leads to out-of-memory errors and event loss.

questions.q: What types of windows are commonly used in streaming aggregations?
options: Tumbling (non-overlapping fixed intervals), sliding (overlapping intervals), and session windows (gap-based grouping of events)
Only tumbling windows can be used in stream processing
Windows are only used in batch processing, not streaming
There is only one type of window used in all streaming systems
questions.explanation: Tumbling windows divide time into fixed non-overlapping segments (e.g., 1-minute intervals). Sliding windows overlap (e.g., 5-minute window advancing every 1 minute). Session windows group events by inactivity gaps (e.g., events within 10 seconds of each other). Each suits different analytical questions.

questions.q: How does a streaming join differ from a batch join in complexity and semantics?
options: Streaming joins must manage unbounded input, out-of-order events, and late arrivals; they require state management and watermarks
Streaming joins are simpler than batch joins because less data is involved
Streaming and batch joins are implemented identically
Streaming joins cannot be performed without converting streams to batches first
questions.explanation: Batch joins have fixed datasets with known bounds. Streaming joins join unbounded, continuously-arriving streams. A stream-stream join requires maintaining state for both sides, handling out-of-order events, and deciding when to emit results. Window boundaries and watermarks become critical for correctness.

questions.q: What is a consumer group in Kafka and why is it important for scaling?
options: Multiple consumers in a group share partitions of a topic, with each partition assigned to one consumer, enabling parallel processing at scale
A consumer group is a single consumer instance with multiple threads
Consumer groups are only used for metadata storage and don't affect processing
All consumers must be in the same group to read from any topic
questions.explanation: A Kafka consumer group allows multiple instances to share the load. If a topic has 10 partitions and 5 consumers in a group, each consumer handles 2 partitions in parallel. If a consumer fails, Kafka rebalances so remaining consumers cover all partitions. This enables horizontal scaling.

questions.q: What does state management in stream processing enable?
options: Maintaining aggregate or contextual data across events (counters, aggregations, join tables) to compute results based on event history
Storing raw events before processing without any computation
Eliminating the need for databases in streaming systems
Reducing the throughput of a streaming pipeline
questions.explanation: State lets streaming apps maintain context. A count aggregation stores current count in state. A sessionization stores recent events grouped by session key. A stream join stores buffered events from one stream waiting for matches in the other. Without state, each event would be processed in isolation.

questions.q: Why might a streaming system use checkpointing?
options: To save processing state periodically so that upon failure, the system resumes from that point without reprocessing or losing events
To validate that all events are correctly formatted before processing
To split large streams into smaller batches
To encrypt data flowing through the pipeline
questions.explanation: Checkpoints capture the system state—current offsets, aggregation results, join state—at regular intervals. If a failure occurs, the system recovers from the latest checkpoint, avoiding reprocessing or dropped events. Without checkpointing, failures require replaying the entire stream history.
